content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
1 FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
1 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
., (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael J. Maurer 
Best Best & Krieger, LLP 
300 South Grand A venue, 25th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 90071 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-091 
Dear Mr. Maurer: 
May 1, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Western Riverside County 
Regional Conservation Authority (the "Authority") Board Member Matt Liesemeyer regarding 
his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict 
of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Authority Board Member Liesemeyer is the President and sole Shareholder of MHL 
Construction and Consulting, Inc. (hereafter "MHL"). MHL is a contract development consultant 
to Murrieta Marketplace Holdings ("MMH"). May Board Member Liesemeyer meet with 
Authority staff on behalf of MHL representing MMH in connection with a donation agreement 
between the Authority and MMH? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Since the Board Member has an interest in MMH, which will be a party to the 
contract at issue, he may not "influence" Authority staff as discussed further below. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are lo the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 2 
We stem Riverside County Regional Conservation Authority: The Authority was formed 
in 2004 to implement the Western Riverside County Multiple Species Habitat Conservation Plan 
(the "Plan") which protects 146 native species of plants, birds, and animals, and preserves a half­
million acres of their habitats. The member cities that have adopted the Plan may require 
developers to dedicate land to the Authority for conservation as mitigation or as a condition of 
approval for a certain project. The Authority also receives funding from development impact fees 
that may be used to purchase land. Therefore, acquiring reserve lands is the Authority's core 
activity. 
• Board of Directors: The Authority is a joint powers authority consisting of 17 member 
cities and the County of Riverside. It is governed by a Board of Directors composed of 
the five members of the Riverside County Board of Supervisors and one elected official 
from each member City. Board Member Liesemeyer is a City Council Member in the 
City of Menifee, and is the City of Menifee's appointee to the Authority Board. 
• Executive Committee: Administrative operations are overseen by a seven-member 
Executive Committee which is made up of members of the the Authority's Board of 
Directors. Board Member Liesemeyer does not serve on the Board's Executive 
Committee. 
• Executive Director: The Authority's operations are carried out by the Executive Director. 
The Authority Board of Directors adopted Ordinance 08-0 l, which authorizes the 
Executive Director to accept gifts or donations on behalf of the Authority. Therefore, the 
Executive Director has authority to accept a dedication of land, upon recommendation by 
staff, without having to obtain the approval of the Board. 
Murrieta Marketplace Holdings: The City of Murrieta is a member of the Authority. 
MMH is in the process of developing property in Murrieta. As a condition of approval of the 
development project, MMH is required to dedicate conservation property to the Authority. As 
´ith all dedications related to the Plan, the Authority will accept the property. The exact terms of 
the dedication will need to be negotiated between the Authority and MMH. 
MHL Construction and Consulting, Inc. ("MHL "): In his private capacity, Board 
Member Liesemeyer is a shareholder (sole owner) and President of MHL. MHL advises property 
owners on the implementation and completion of development projects, overseeing such 
necessary activities as the preparation of plans and specifications, the selection and supervision 
of contractors, the development of project budgets, the processing of permits and entitlements, 
and management of construction. MHL serves as a development consultant to MMH and as the 
project consultant, one of MHL's duties is to negotiate this donation agreement with the 
Authority. MHL has received compensation from MMH within the past 12 months. 


File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 3 
You have asked whether Board Member Liesemeyer, as President and Shareholder of 
MHL, may meet with the Authority staff to negotiate a donation agreement on behalf MMH. In 
making this request, you note: 
I. Board Member Liesemeyer performs no official duties on behalf of the Authority 
related to the donation, because the decision to enter the donation agreement is solely within the 
Executive Director's authority. The Authority Board will not be making or performing any role 
in any governmental decision. Board Member Liesemeyer's role in any meetings related to the 
donation will be solely in his private capacity and not as a Board Member. Thus, he will not be 
contacting the Executive Director or anyone else within the Authority to influence a decision in 
his capacity as Board Member. 
2. You believe that Board Member Liesemeyer's role is essentially analogous to the 
situation discussed in Regulation 18704(d)(6) regarding drawings or submissions of an 
architectural, engineering, or similar nature prepared by a public official for a client to submit in 
a proceeding before the official's own agency. Though this situation does not involve the 
submittal of drawings, you state that Board Member Liesemeyer is simply assisting his client to 
process an entitlement requirement, pursuant to Board Member Liesemeyer's profession, and his 
contact with agency staff is limited to discussions of the process and an evaluation of the 
sufficiency of the donation. Moreover, as the sole shareholder of MHL, Board Member 
Liesemeyer' s appearance on behalf of MMH is necessary. 
3. Finally, you also note that there does not appear to be any material financial effect on 
MMH. In accordance with the Plan, and as a condition of approval imposed by the City of 
Murrieta, MMH is required to donate land to the Authority. Likewise, in accordance with the 
Plan and with the Authority's mission, the Authority will accept the donation. The terms of any 
related agreement simply implement these requirements. 
On April 18, 2017, you provided the Authority's standard form template and the 
following additional facts. 
1. The only parties to the actual donation agreement are the authority and the developer. 
However, there are three steps in the process: 
• The developer submits a project application to the City, and the City determines and 
advises the developer as whether the Plan applies and whether a donation will be required 
as a condition of project approval. The City's determination is guided by the "HANS" -
the Habitat Evaluation and Acquisition Negotiations Strategy - which applies to property 
that may be included in the Plan and provides standards for determining the scope of a 
required donation. The City's determination is based on its application of the Plan and the 
HANS. 
• There is a joint project review where the City refers its determination to the Authority 
administrative staff to obtain the staffs concurrence or comments as to whether the 
project complies with the Plan. 


File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 4 
• Once the City officially determines that a donation to the Authority will be necessary, the 
developer then must enter into a donation agreement with the Authority as a condition of 
project approval. Thus, the Authority and the developer do not negotiate the scope of the 
donation because that has already been determined by the City. The executive director, 
not the Board, enters the agreement on behalf of the Authority. 
The City is the approval authority of the development application and is the entity that 
conditions approval on don_ation of land to the the Authority. The developer is the owner of the 
project. In order for the project to be approved, the developer must meet all conditions imposed 
by the City. The Authority's only role is to hold and maintain donated property for habitat 
conservation in perpetuity. The only role that the Authority's staff has in the development 
process is to enter into the donation agreement and to receive the donation. The Authority Board 
has no role in the development process and does not discuss or take action on donations. 
2. The property to be donated is already fixed before a donation agreement is considered 
by the Authority. The legal description of the property is already determined by the City and is 
not negotiated between the developef and the Authority. The requirement to use the property_ for 
conservation is recorded as part of a final map in accordance with the Subdivision Map Act, and 
this recording prohibits any other use of the property. The City would have to take action, 
pursuant to the Subdivision Map Act, to revise the recorded final map and allow the property to 
be put to a use other than conservation purposes. Therefore, the amount or location of the 
donated property is set before a donation agreement is considered. Developers may negotiate 
minor changes to the other terms. At the City level, the City's determination of the property to be 
donated is essentially fixed by the Plan and the process for determining the donation is controlled 
by the HANS. 
3. The donation agreement itself does not directly financially impact the developer 
because there is no decision being made in the donation agreement that affects the land to be 
donated. The amount of land is based on the conditions of approval that the City imposes, in 
accordance with the Plan. The final map, approved and recorded by the City, restricts the 
donation property to only be used for conservation purposes. The property to be donated is 
therefore set in stone by the final map, and it is already required to be utilized for the donation. 
The amount or location of land to be donated is not negotiated as part of the donation agreement. 
4. The donation agreement is a contract. 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Reform Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or 


File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 5 
consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Financial 
interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103 as follows: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b ). ) An indirect interest in business or real property means a 
business or real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official. 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a 
spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the 
official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent or 
greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $470 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87 I03(e).) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Based on your facts, Board Member Liesemeyer has a business interest in MHL pursuant to 
Section 87103(a) and (d) and as a source of income under Section 87 I03(c). In addition, the 
clients of MHL would be considered a source of income to him, including MMH. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved 
in a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named 
party in, or subject of, a governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) MMH is explicitly 
involved in the governmental decision at issue since the agreement will be between the Authority 
and MMH. 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards for a decision's effect on an 
official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)( 1) provides: 
"[T]he financial effect is material if: 
"( l) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, 
or is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding ... " 


File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 6 
Note that when the source of income is explicitly involved in a decision, as here, the effect is 
deemed material. 
Disqualification under Sections 87100 and 87103. 
As noted above, the Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, 
participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision 
in which the official has a financial interest. Regulation 18704 sets forth the following: 
• 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" if the official authorizes or directs any 
action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of 
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(Regulation 18704(a).) 
• A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" if the official provides 
information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision 
without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b).) 
• Public officials are also prohibited from "influencing" a governmental decision. As 
applicable to the Board Member, the Act prohibits the Board Member from contacting, or 
appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence, any member, officer, employee, or 
co11s11/tant of his agency or any agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his 
agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(l).) 
Appearing before the Authority (and its staff) falls under the prohibition in Regulation 
l 8704(c)( l) above.2 Thus, since the Commissioner would have a conflict of interest, he would 
not be permitted to contact, or appear before, or otherwise influence, any member, officer, 
employee, or consultant of the Authority. 
Exceptions 
1. Regulation 18702(b) cautions: "Notwithstanding Regulations 18702. l through 
18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, 
inconsequential, or insignificant." As noted above, while the primary donation terms are set 
before it gets to the Authority, the negotiable terms would be interlinked to the entirety of 
donation agreement. Terms cannot be segmented from the agreement as a whole. And the 
Q "For purposes of determining whether a public official will be influencing a governmental decision 
through his or her communications or meetings with city staff, it is important to realize that staff does not exist in a 
vacuum; it is not a generic entity with its own identity. Rather, staff is a component of the agency to which it is 
assigned. An 'agency' includes the staff assigned exclusively to a public official's agency (Larmore Advice Letter, 
No. A-00-275; Martello Advice Letter, No. A-85-190) as well as staff that are shared between that and another 
agency (West Advice Leuer, No. A-88-413)." (Farrell Advice Leuer, No. 1-03-121) 


File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 7 
entirety of the agreement would have a financial effect on the developer in a significant way. 
Therefore, this exception would not apply.3 
2. Regulation l 8704(d) provides (as pertinent to your facts) the following exceptions: 
"( l) Ministerial. Actions by a public official that are solely ministerial, 
secretarial, or clerical." 
We have advised that a decision is ministerial where there is no discretion. "The Act does 
provide an exception for "ministerial" decisions .... Ministerial actions do not constitute the 
making or participating in making of a governmental decision because they do not involve any 
discretion on the part of the official. (See, Brown Advice Letter, No. 1-02-026; Halm Advice 
Letter, No. 1-91-037.) While your facts show that the primary terms of the agreement are set 
before the proposal is considered by Authority staff, you also noted that Developers may 
negotiate other changes to the terms. Thus, the negotiation of the agreement would not appear to 
be ministerial or without discretion. 
"(6) Architectural and Engineering Documents. 
"(A) Drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering, or 
similar nature prepared by a public official for a client to submit in a 
proceeding before the official's agency if: 
"(i) The work is performed pursuant to the official's profession; and 
"(ii) The official does not make any contact with the agency other than 
contact with agency staff concerning the process or evaluation of the 
documents prepared by the official." 
You suggested in your request that the Board Member's role is essentially analogous to 
the situation discussed in Regulation 18704(d)(6) regarding drawings or submissions of an 
architectural, engineering, or similar nature prepared by a public official for a client to submit in 
a proceeding before the official's own agency. You stated that though this situation does not 
involve the submittal of drawings, you state that Board Member Liesemeyer is simply assisting 
his client to process an entitlement requirement, pursuant to Board Member Liesemeyer's 
profession, and his contact with agency staff is limited to discussions of the process and an 
evaluation of the sufficiency of the donation. 
Exceptions, including those in the Act, are construed narrowly. (See, Julius Goldman's 
Egg City v. Air Pollution Control Dept. of Ventura County (1981) 116 Cal.App.3d 741.) In this 
case, your facts do not fit the substantive terms of the exception and therefore it would not apply. 
3 Regulation 18706 provides that related decisions are not segmentablc if they arc inextricably interrelated. 
Regulation l 8706(b) states: "For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result 
of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 


Section 1090 
File No. A-17-091 
Page No. 8 
You have not asked about, and we do not consider, Section 1090. But your question 
concerns a contract so you should consider Section 1090 or request further advice if you believe 
it may apply. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
ant General Counsel, 
Division

sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or  
consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Financial 
interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103 as follows: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b ). ) An indirect interest in business or real property means a 
business or real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official. 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a 
spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the 
official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent or 
greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $470 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87 I03(e).) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Based on your facts, Board Member Liesemeyer has a business interest in MHL pursuant to 
Section 87103(a) and (d) and as a source of income under Section 87 I03(c). In addition, the 
clients of MHL would be considered a source of income to him, including MMH. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved 
in a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named 
party in, or subject of, a governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) MMH is explicitly 
involved in the governmental decision at issue since the agreement will be between the Authority 
and MMH. 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards for a decision's effect on an 
official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)( 1) provides: 
"[T]he financial effect is material if: 
"( l) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, 
or is otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding ... "  
Note that when the source of income is explicitly involved in a decision, as here, the effect is 
deemed material. 
Disqualification under Sections 87100 and 87103. 
As noted above, the Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, 
participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision 
in which the official has a financial interest. Regulation 18704 sets forth the following: 
• 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" if the official authorizes or directs any 
action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of 
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(Regulation 18704(a).) 
• A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" if the official provides 
information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision 
without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b).) 
• Public officials are also prohibited from "influencing" a governmental decision. As 
applicable to the Board Member, the Act prohibits the Board Member from contacting, or 
appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence, any member, officer, employee, or 
co11s11/tant of his agency or any agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his 
agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(l).) 
Appearing before the Authority (and its staff) falls under the prohibition in Regulation 
l 8704(c)( l) above.2 Thus, since the Commissioner would have a conflict of interest, he would 
not be permitted to contact, or appear before, or otherwise influence, any member, officer, 
employee, or consultant of the Authority. 
Exceptions 
1. Regulation 18702(b) cautions: "Notwithstanding Regulations 18702. l through 
18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, 
inconsequential, or insignificant." As noted above, while the primary donation terms are set 
before it gets to the Authority, the negotiable terms would be interlinked to the entirety of 
donation agreement. Terms cannot be segmented from the agreement as a whole. And the 
Q "For purposes of determining whether a public official will be influencing a governmental decision 
through his or her communications or meetings with city staff, it is important to realize that staff does not exist in a 
vacuum; it is not a generic entity with its own identity. Rather, staff is a component of the agency to which it is 
assigned. An 'agency' includes the staff assigned exclusively to a public official's agency (Larmore Advice Letter, 
No. A-00-275; Martello Advice Letter, No. A-85-190) as well as staff that are shared between that and another 
agency (West Advice Leuer, No. A-88-413)." (Farrell Advice Leuer, No. 1-03-121)  
entirety of the agreement would have a financial effect on the developer in a significant way. 
Therefore, this exception would not apply.3 
2. Regulation l 8704(d) provides (as pertinent to your facts) the following exceptions: 
"( l) Ministerial. Actions by a public official that are solely ministerial, 
secretarial, or clerical." 
We have advised that a decision is ministerial where there is no discretion. "The Act does 
provide an exception for "ministerial" decisions .... Ministerial actions do not constitute the 
making or participating in making of a governmental decision because they do not involve any 
discretion on the part of the official. (See, Brown Advice Letter, No. 1-02-026; Halm Advice 
Letter, No. 1-91-037.) While your facts show that the primary terms of the agreement are set 
before the proposal is considered by Authority staff, you also noted that Developers may 
negotiate other changes to the terms. Thus, the negotiation of the agreement would not appear to 
be ministerial or without discretion. 
"(6) Architectural and Engineering Documents. 
"(A) Drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering, or 
similar nature prepared by a public official for a client to submit in a 
proceeding before the official's agency if: 
"(i) The work is performed pursuant to the official's profession; and 
"(ii) The official does not make any contact with the agency other than 
contact with agency staff concerning the process or evaluation of the 
documents prepared by the official." 
You suggested in your request that the Board Member's role is essentially analogous to 
the situation discussed in Regulation 18704(d)(6) regarding drawings or submissions of an 
architectural, engineering, or similar nature prepared by a public official for a client to submit in 
a proceeding before the official's own agency. You stated that though this situation does not 
involve the submittal of drawings, you state that Board Member Liesemeyer is simply assisting 
his client to process an entitlement requirement, pursuant to Board Member Liesemeyer's 
profession, and his contact with agency staff is limited to discussions of the process and an 
evaluation of the sufficiency of the donation. 
Exceptions, including those in the Act, are construed narrowly. (See, Julius Goldman's 
Egg City v. Air Pollution Control Dept. of Ventura County (1981) 116 Cal.App.3d 741.) In this 
case, your facts do not fit the substantive terms of the exception and therefore it would not apply. 
3 Regulation 18706 provides that related decisions are not segmentablc if they arc inextricably interrelated. 
Regulation l 8706(b) states: "For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result 
of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 


Section 1090  
You have not asked about, and we do not consider, Section 1090. But your question 
concerns a contract so you should consider Section 1090 or request further advice if you believe 
it may apply.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Authority Board Member Liesemeyer is the President and sole Shareholder of MHL 
Construction and Consulting, Inc. (hereafter "MHL"). MHL is a contract development consultant 
to Murrieta Marketplace Holdings ("MMH"). May Board Member Liesemeyer meet with 
Authority staff on behalf of MHL representing MMH in connection with a donation agreement 
between the Authority and MMH?

CONCLUSION: No. Since the Board Member has an interest in MMH, which will be a party to the 
contract at issue, he may not "influence" Authority staff as discussed further below.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA 1 FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 1 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 ., (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Michael J. Maurer Best Best & Krieger, LLP 300 South Grand A venue, 25th Floor Los Angeles, CA 90071 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-091 Dear Mr. Maurer: May 1, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Western Riverside County Regional Conservation Authority (the "Authority") Board Member Matt Liesemeyer regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTION Authority Board Member Liesemeyer is the President and sole Shareholder of MHL Construction and Consulting, Inc. (hereafter "MHL"). MHL is a contract development consultant to Murrieta Marketplace Holdings ("MMH"). May Board Member Liesemeyer meet with Authority staff on behalf of MHL representing MMH in connection with a donation agreement between the Authority and MMH? CONCLUSION No. Since the Board Member has an interest in MMH, which will be a party to the contract at issue, he may not "influence" Authority staff as discussed further below. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are lo the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS File No. A-17-091 Page No. 2 We stem Riverside County Regional Conservation Authority: The Authority was formed in 2004 to implement the Western Riverside County Multiple Species Habitat Conservation Plan (the "Plan") which protects 146 native species of plants, birds, and animals, and preserves a half­ million acres of their habitats. The member cities that have adopted the Plan may require developers to dedicate land to the Authority for conservation as mitigation or as a condition of approval for a certain project. The Authority also receives funding from development impact fees that may be used to purchase land. Therefore, acquiring reserve lands is the Authority's core activity. • Board of Directors: The Authority is a joint powers authority consisting of 17 member cities and the County of Riverside. It is governed by a Board of Directors composed of the five members of the Riverside County Board of Supervisors and one elected official from each member City. Board Member Liesemeyer is a City Council Member in the City of Menifee, and is the City of Menifee's appointee to the Authority Board. • Executive Committee: Administrative operations are overseen by a seven-member Executive Committee which is made up of members of the the Authority's Board of Directors.
